summary: Athanasius of Alexandria (c. 296-373 CE) was bishop of Alexandria and a leading defender of the Nicene confession that the Son is homoousios (of the same essence) with the Father. He is the author of On the Incarnation and of works against Arianism.
description: Athanasius served as bishop of Alexandria from 328 until his death, a tenure interrupted by repeated exiles arising from the controversies over the Council of Nicaea (325). He became the most prominent defender of the Nicene position against what later tradition grouped as Arianism, the range of views associated with Arius holding that the Son is a creature, made by the Father and not co-eternal or of the same essence with him. Athanasius argued that the Son is homoousios (of the same essence) with the Father, and that only if the Son is fully divine can he save and deify humanity. In his early double work Against the Gentiles and On the Incarnation he set out an account of creation, the fall, and the incarnation of the Word (Logos), arguing that the Word became human so that humans might be restored to the knowledge and image of God. In later polemical writings, including the Orations against the Arians and letters defending the Nicene creed, he developed the exegetical and theological case for the full divinity of the Son and, subsequently, of the Holy Spirit. Modern scholarship has read a central coherence in his thought in the distinction and simultaneous relation between God and the created world, which orders his doctrines of creation, Christology, and salvation. His defense of Nicaea, often against imperial and episcopal opposition, gave rise to the later epithet Athanasius contra mundum (Athanasius against the world).
editorial_notes: Birth and death years (c. 296-373 CE) are approximate; the birth year in particular is uncertain and is given with c. in the lifespan note. Athanasius is described here on Christian terms, per neutrality principle #6. 'Arianism' is used as the later heresiological label for a range of positions; the entry states the disputed claim (whether the Son is created and non-co-eternal, or homoousios with the Father) as the matter in dispute and attributes the Nicene position to Athanasius rather than adjudicating it, in keeping with the policy against flattening a live debate. An earlier draft carried a cross-tradition compares_with link from Athanasius to the Islamic concept khalq-al-quran (the createdness of the Qur'an). That comparison has been consolidated onto the arianism entry, the position that affirms the createdness of the Son, which is the tighter same-polarity parallel to the createdness of the Qur'an, so the created-or-uncreated comparison is not double-counted across two Christian entries. Sources triangulate three lenses per neutrality principle #8: (a) a modern academic monograph (Anatolios, Routledge), (b) Athanasius's own primary text (On the Incarnation), and (c) a contemporary theologian's treatment of the controversy (Rowan Williams, Arius). Confidence is medium: sources verified to exist and be on-topic via publisher and library catalogues and peer-reviewed reviews, but relevant passages not read line-by-line.

lifespan.note: c. 296-373 CE; bishop of Alexandria from 328. Birth year uncertain and approximate.

relations.note: Defended the Nicene confession that the Son is of the same essence (homoousios) with the Father; reported as his attributed position.
Opposed the range of views grouped as Arianism, which held the Son to be a creature; the critique is attributed to him, not adjudicated.
Set out an account of the incarnation of the Word in On the Incarnation, arguing the Word became human so humans might be restored to God.
His defense of the full divinity of the Son, and later of the Spirit, contributed to the developing Nicene doctrine of the Trinity.

sources.citation: Anatolios, Khaled. Athanasius: The Coherence of His Thought. Routledge Early Church Monographs. London and New York: Routledge, 1998.

sources.citation: Athanasius of Alexandria. On the Incarnation (De Incarnatione). Translated by John Behr. Popular Patristics Series. Yonkers, NY: St Vladimir's Seminary Press, 2011. Primary source: Athanasius's own account of the incarnation of the Word.
